title: Attributes
text:
GoalkeepersWing BacksCentral DefendersMidfieldersForwards



ShotStopping: The ability to prevent goals from shots on target through sharp reflexes, positioning, and reach.
Passing: The quality and reliability of short and medium distribution with the feet to safely participate in team buildup play.
LongBalls: The accuracy and distance of direct, long-range distributions and clearances aimed at turning over territory.
CrossStopping: Effectiveness in dealing with crosses and high balls into the box through catching, punching, and decisive movement.
Sweeping: The ability to read the play, leave the penalty box, and intercept through balls or clear danger behind a high defensive line.




Scoring: Efficiency in converting attacking chances, back-post runs, or loose balls into actual goals.
ShotAcc: The accuracy of shots taken, measuring precision and disciplined shot selection over raw shot volume.
Passing: The accuracy and consistency of short and medium passes used to maintain possession and recycle play out wide.
Progression: The overall ability to advance play down the flanks via progressive passes and line-breaking carries.
Creativity: The ability to generate scoring chances from wide or deep areas through vision, overlapping runs, or clever interchanges.
Assisting: Direct contribution to goals via final balls, cutbacks, or standard assists.
Crossing: The quality, shape, and precision of aerial or low balls delivered into the opposition penalty area from wide zones.
Dribbling: The ability to beat defensive pressure, win individual wide duels, and safely carry possession forward.
Tackling: The timing and success of defensive challenges to cleanly win the ball without committing wide fouls.
Blocking: The ability to block crosses, shots, and passing lanes through proactive positioning and body control.
Heading: Success and effectiveness in aerial duels, both defensively at the back post and offensively during transitions.
Pressing: The frequency and intensity of defensive actions meant to disrupt the opponent’s build-up high up the flank.




Shooting: The overall threat and accuracy of shots taken, typically from deep positions, set plays, or late arrivals.
Passing: The basic accuracy and consistency of passes used to securely maintain possession across the defensive line.
Progression: The combined ability to carry the ball forward or execute progressive vertical passes into the midfield.
Creativity: The ability to split open defensive blocks from deep areas through vision or unexpected line-breaking passes.
Assisting: Direct contribution to goals via long-range assists, set-piece headers, or key deep playmaking actions.
Crossing: The execution of long, sweeping switches of play to isolated fullbacks or wingers on the opposite flank.
Tackling: The timing, cleanliness, and success rate of defensive challenges to stop attackers without conceding central fouls.
Blocking: The ability to block central shots and intercept forward passing lanes through high spatial anticipation.
Heading: Dominance and success rate in structural aerial duels, crucial for clearing standard crosses and controlling set pieces.




Scoring: Efficiency in converting midfield chances, long-range efforts, or calculated box-arrivals into goals.
ShotAcc: The accuracy of open-play shots, maximizing the efficiency of long-range attempts or late runs.
Passing: The accuracy and reliability of short and medium passes used to cleanly circulate possession and dictate game tempo.
LongBalls: The accuracy and vision required to execute long-range switches that flip the point of attack or launch deep transitions.
Progression: The overall capacity to move the ball forward into the opponent’s territory via progressive passes and central carries.
Creativity: The ability to generate goal-scoring chances through vision, imagination, and clever decision-making in tight spaces.
Assisting: Direct contribution to goals through precise final passes, tracking timing and execution in the final third.
Crossing: The quality and accuracy of deliveries into the penalty box when drifting into wide spaces or taking set-pieces.
Dribbling: The ability to beat opponents under pressure, carry the ball smoothly through midfield, and break defensive shapes.
Tackling: The effectiveness and timing of challenges to disrupt the opponent’s rhythm and kill transitions early.
Blocking: The ability to cut off central passing lanes and shield the defense through smart positioning and anticipation.
Pressing: The work rate and efficiency displayed when closing down opponents to disrupt their possession and force turnovers.




Scoring: Efficiency and composure in front of goal to consistently convert attacking opportunities into actual goals.
ShotAcc: The accuracy of shots on target, measuring placement and disciplined shot selection over raw volume.
LongBalls: The execution of long switches or cross-field distributions when dropping deep to link up the team’s play.
Passing: The quality and reliability of short passes, lay-offs, and quick combinations in highly congested attacking spaces.
Progression: The ability to move play forward under pressure, primarily reflecting ball-carrying threat rather than build-up duties.
Creativity: The ability to create scoring opportunities for others through vision, movement, and improvisation in the attacking third.
Assisting: Direct contribution to goals via assists, showing spatial awareness and choosing to pass when shooting is suboptimal.
Crossing: The accuracy of deliveries or low cutbacks sent into the box from the channels or wide attacking areas.
Dribbling: The ability to beat central or wide defenders one-on-one, create immediate separation, and destabilize shapes.
Heading: Efficiency in winning aerial duels in the final third to secure long balls, knock down layoffs, or score headers.
Pressing: The intensity and tactical discipline shown when leading the first line of defense to disrupt the opponent’s build-up.

title: Description
text:
Installation
# Install remotes in case you don't have it
install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("machoude/DataScouteR")

# Load the library
library(DataScouteR)

title: Description
text:
Players
gk <- get_gk()
head(gk)

dc <- get_dc()
head(dc)

wb <- get_wb()
head(wb)

cm <- get_cm()
head(cm)

w <- get_w()
head(w)

fw <- get_fw()
head(fw)

title: Description
text:
Methodology
The workflow behind the package follows these principles:

Player-level effects are filtered to reduce noise from small samples
League-level effects are included to implicitly account for competition strength

This scaling is not intended to replicate Football Manager internally, but rather to:

Provide a familiar reference frame
Improve interpretability for practitioners

title: Description
text:
Why a 1–20 Scale?
The 1–20 scale:

Preserves relative differences between players
Compresses extreme values smoothly
Allows quick qualitative interpretation (e.g. elite, above average, poor)

This makes the results more accessible for:

Scouting discussions
Player profiling
Comparative analysis across leagues

title: Description
text:
Interpreting the Metrics
The ratings in DataScouteR are scaled from 1 to 20 FM-style for easier comparison. A player’s score shows how well they performed this season, relative to other players in the dataset.
For example, if a goalkeeper has a 16 in ShotStopping while Alisson has 8, it does not mean the player is generally better than Alisson. It only indicates that, in the current season, this player performed better in shot-stopping relative to the other goalkeepers in the dataset.
Also get_fw() and get_mc() use similar metrics, the ratings are position-specific. A Scoring rating of 15 for a midfielder does not carry the same meaning as a 15 for a forward, since all scores are calculated relative to players in the same position group.
This scale is designed for seasonal comparison, not historical ranking. High numbers mean above-average performance this year, while lower numbers indicate below-average performance for the same season.
